speaker: interviewee
text: --Weiler. This is tape 10. I was made aware by the interviewer that I'm allowed to leave a message, which will consist of two parts-- one for the general public who may be interested, and one to the family. Some of the words may be strong, but I have, as you can imagine, quite strong feelings on this subject. First of all, we-- and I call the survivors-- are not mentally demented people, as many people think. We are not freaks. Yes, we have suffered. Yes, we mourn the memory of our parents, of our grandparents, of our brothers and sisters and friends, and all those millions who we didn't even know and have perished. Every day of our lives-- not only once a year, as it is customary in the Jewish religion when people remember the dead-- have we remembered and thought of all those people who were tortured, killed-- every day, of not one year, but more than one year. So it is proper to observe every day of the year. When we look back and take stock what the Holocaust was about and what we should learn from it, I should say, we should never give up. We must be vigilant at all times to the dangers to the Jewish people as it has existed in most generations before us. There has always been, and will be, individuals, groups, governments who will perpetrate and have perpetrated atrocities in the name of their religion or their political agenda. Whose aim was, is, and will be to assimilate us, to change our beliefs, alternatively, restrict our freedom, excluding us from higher forms of education and choice of occupation, eventually leading to the well-known false charges and accusations being made that we are only parasites, moneylenders, and infidels, confiscate our assets and homes, drive us out from the towns, villages, dehumanize us, and finally, destroy us physically. We are the survivors. Our children, and grandchildren, and older generations to follow are not and never will have the right to forgive, as we are not entitled, even after 50 years or even ever, to forgive the deaths of millions of victims who were put to death in the most cruel manner. I go further to add that we are and were all decent people, and there will be decent people in the world at large. We owe it to the memory of all those who have perished, and it is only the victims, the dead, who could forgive. But even we can't ask them, as we don't even know where to find their graves, as they were even denied to have the right of a final resting place.

speaker: interviewee
text: I find it intolerable, if adding insult to injury, to hear these days that war criminals, mass murderers should not be pursued, as after all, they are old, frail people, and what good would it do? After all, they've only killed 5, 10, 15 men, women, and children. It is interesting how prominent, intelligent people, church leaders, humanists, animal lovers treat the memory of innocent martyrs as if it would be a numbers game. These peaceful, easily forgiving people take such a cavalier attitude, as if it would have been done to people who didn't have the right to exist. I wonder what their attitude may have been if it would have been done to their families. I wonder, and I need some convincing-- how much good intention is behind the forgiving attitude?

speaker: interviewee
text: We must resist and not permit our individual and the existence of the Jewish people, now or ever, should be concerned by or categorized by others as a disposable commodity. For the good of other religions and nations, as it is really better, maybe, without the Jews. It is a difficult subject, but we are the people of the book. We have contributed more to the world than most other nations. Present or future Jewish generations should remember not to make the same mistake as most of our well-meaning, naive contemporaries made, that this or that cannot happen to us, as we are law-abiding, good citizens of our country of birth, and put our entire trust in the hands of others in exchange of some sort of verbal or written paper of assurance, and that all will be done for us. And we all-- what we have to do is to remain patient and wait. Our contemporary history has proven beyond doubt that by self-delusion and a policy of appeasement, and expecting criminals, Jew-haters to change their attitude by peaceful means only leads little by little to the horrific events of our experiences. This can happen again and again, maybe on a different continent or a country, and it may take a different form. Our history is there to prove it. It happened before. It may happen again. Religious intolerance, greed, was the powerhouse which generated the Holocaust, but there is a great deal to be answered by many church leaders who have sown the seeds of hatred and lies for years and decades and centuries from the pulpits in their churches in the name of glory of God. They have created the climate for the scum of political parties and leaders to finish the job they have so carefully nurtured, engineered, and created the climate to exterminate us. This is my message to the public in general. I do not think I'm being hard on anyone. I believe that my feelings are my own. I'm entitled to my opinion. And I can only say probably, if I would have lived in another part of the world and I wouldn't have seen what I have at the early age, I may have taken a much more benign and pleasant attitude. My dear family, I say to you, always invest in your and your children's education, Jewish and secular. Excel. Hold on to your heritage, beliefs. Be proud of our people. Learn from the past. Be proud of what you do, but not-- but not conceited. Do not hint our adversaries-- the pleasure to rejoice in spite of what they have done to us. And do not let yourself be broken emotionally or physically. Be realistic, but not overconfident. Focus your mind on the important principles. Have your foot firmly on the ground. Never believe that danger does not exist and does not lurk around the corner-- only you may not be aware of it. The ideal world, unfortunately, only exists in books and theories. Your reality is different. To my dear wife, two children, son-in-law, daughter-in-law, and grandchildren, I say, before I close, the following note. Soldier on bravely. Life is not meant to be easy. It is worth fighting for. And thank you very much, Mr. Steven Spielberg, for giving the opportunity and possibility making the film of the which I think created the whole project to be started on. As far as I understand, the proceeds are donated for making up the financial cost of these interviews. And I only say thank you very much. I think you're doing a worthwhile job. I must say that I was in Los Angeles in 1980 with my family, and I happened to have eaten in Milky Way, which, to the best of my knowledge, was Mr. Spielberg's mother's [? dairy ?] restaurant. Other than that, there are probably many things, but I tried to condense the story-- at least my message. And I want to make a final point, which I have not made a note of. I mentioned it to my interviewer, Sharon, and it goes as follows. I lived in a Catholic country. I have no intention to challenge 650 million Catholics around the world. We need them. There are nice people amongst them, and so on. There is a serious question regarding the Vatican's attitude during the Holocaust. There's no question about it that Pious XII was a German sympathizer, if not a Nazi supporter.

speaker: interviewee
text: The excuse not to save Jews was we can't take the risk because Hitler will march in on the Vatican and will cause harm to our Catholic church. At a point, I'm prepared to be generous and concede the point that maybe that was the reason. But my belief is not so infinite in the statement, because I would ever like to ask an answer to my question, why was it the Vatican who, in 1945, actively-- what was known as the Vatican route-- with Peron of Argentina conspired to save the worst criminals-- Mengele, Eichmann, and so on?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it is a known fact that the main culprits have escaped to South America, to Paraguay, which was a similar state as Argentina. And there is no excuse for that because they did not have to worry about Hitler after May '45. And I would like to have an answer any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Weiler, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure. And maybe one more point that is important. Again, I didn't write it down. I feel terribly sorry, and I mean it-- my heart goes out-- to all those people who I've seen in interviews like that who have unfortunately broken down to the point-- people of eminence and respectable intelligence-- and have not been able hardly get through the words through their mouth. It is very unfortunate. I feel very sorry for them. I'm fortunate enough that I survived. I'm still in full faculty of my mind, and I would not want to hand the least feeling of victory that they have been able to break us. We survived, and we will survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. These are a set of photographs which I got back after the war with the help of my cousin, as we had absolutely nothing left. And I would like to explain to you the period from when they were taken and who they are. Going from left to right, the upper large picture-- the two people are my parents, Sigmund Weiler and Elvira [INAUDIBLE], Mrs. Weiler. It was taken in 1922 on their wedding day. The next picture, going to the right with three adults and two children are, on the left, my father, Sigmund Weiler, approximately in 1937, '38, in a holiday resort called Heviz, which was a spa near the Balaton Lake. The next one to him is my older brother, who was born in 1924. Then my mother and her father. That is my maternal grandfather, Martin Holzer, or [INAUDIBLE]. And the small boy in the front is myself. Going on the upper end of the small picture with the mother and two children, that's my mother, my brother behind her, and I'm on my mother's lap. In the last picture, singularly standing is myself at presumably the age of 4 or something similar to that. Below my mother and my brother's picture is a small picture, a little boy with two poodles or toys in his hand. That is my brother at an early age. And then the next picture is again my brother, Irvin, which I recovered, of his Matura matriculation board of the school in the 1980s. Coming on the lower level, the first-- the young boy in the naval suit is my brother. And the next one is my brother, Irvin, and myself on his lap. The exact ages must have been similar to the one from above. Now, the large group photo on the right lower end is my Weiler family. These are-- sitting down in the center is my late grandmother, Kathleen Weiler, and her husband, [INAUDIBLE] Weiler. Starting on the left, the gentleman sitting there is my father's one older brother, [? Julius ?] Weiler, who was the father of Gershon Weiler, the professor who I referred to in my recording. Next to him standing is the lady Sidonia Weiler. She was the oldest. She was married to a gentleman called [INAUDIBLE] or [? Julius ?] [INAUDIBLE], who had the shoe shop which I mentioned. Next to her, the gentleman looking sideways is Dr. Bela Weiler, the lawyer, who was the president of the Jewish community, and about whom I also mentioned. Next to him is the youngest sister, Margot Weiler-- married [INAUDIBLE]. She is the only one who is alive today. On the 29th of April, 1996, was her 97th birthday. She is in an old people's home because unfortunately, a year and a half ago she had a stroke in Melbourne, Australia in the Montefiore home. Next to her, standing, looking sideways, is my father, Sigmund or [INAUDIBLE] Weiler, who I have also referred to in my recording. And sitting with the folded arm in front of him on the far right is his younger brother, Eugene-- Dr. Eugene Weiler, who was the doctor who I also referred in the previous records. On the left-hand of this eight-picture shot, there are, on the lower left-hand picture, two gravestones. These are the gravestones of my Weiler grandparents, who died in the late '20s. I personally didn't know them because I was born in 1930. The picture next to it-- the brick wall with a tablet only-- that is, so to speak, a memorial plaque recording the place of burial of all the Sefer Torahs and other parchment, religious articles, which were destroyed or defaced by the Nazis or Hungarians or Germans. The other five pictures-- that is, the upper three and the lower two-- are photographs of the deportation memorial, which is erected as a separate hall in the Jewish cemetery of Sopron, denoting all of the names of the people who were deported but have not returned. There is an additional plaque, which is not visible here, which records, also, some people named by their Jewish names. It must be noted that the cemetery can be visited because there is a caretaker still there, in spite of the fact that there's hardly any Jews. There may be two or three Jews left, of very old age. This is one of the documents, which, as a hobby, maybe, I collected. This is a document from the county of Sopron [INAUDIBLE]-- that's the place where the Weiler family originated from-- describing all the residents of that small hamlet from 1850. And you will note that in the fourth line, number 38 is a name of Veiler, [? Gospar. ?] Even so, it's spelled with a V and not a W. And on the vertical lines, it describes what nationality and so on they are. And he is under name of Jewish as being a resident of that place in 1850 and being a property owner. This is a copy of a document which I acquired in the late '80s when I visited Sopron, as I wasn't there for 30 years. Which is basically, on the top in Hebrew, written my Weiler grandfather's name, [INAUDIBLE] Weiler. And it's called [INAUDIBLE], which means, like, a charitable trust, which my father and his brothers established in the town of Sopron in honor of their father and mother, whose names are on it, and also various memorial, as it is normal when such charitable foundations are established. So basically, this is a family document after the deaths of my grandparents the family has established. These are two photographs, which I received from my cousin, professor Gershon Weiler. He has done a great deal of research of the family's background and records. And he took this photograph in one of the cemeteries in Burgenland, Austria, which is around in the same district as our city was. And on the other picture, it is himself in an orchard because as an Israeli citizen, he was not allowed to enter Hungary all the years during the communist times. The actual gravestone describes a Rabbi, Weiler, who he thought was a relative of our family, who obviously also lived many years ago in that area. This set of four photos are from Sopron, which were taken during the communist times when I had no intention of going back. And the first photo under the sign of Sopron is my wife when, on my information, she went back. Has never lived in Hungary. The next photo alongside it is a wall showing a land where our house used to stand, which was bombed during late '44 or early '45. Below is the same place, except it is showing the next door house, which was practically-- our house was practically identical to one. Finally, the right-hand bottom corner shows the orthodox synagogue of Sopron, which I knew very well. After the deportation, all its furnishings have been ripped out, and the local Nazis used it as an animal store. And it was never really put into use after the war. And presently, it has become beyond repair, so I wouldn't give it very long before we will not see it again. This picture was taken by a non-Jew in the time of May, June, 1944 in [INAUDIBLE], which was one of the ghettos. You can see in the front the metal chain, which later, a timber fence was added. It must have been taken by one of the local people. My cousin, Gershon Weiler, who had a lifelong interest in the history of the Holocaust, has given it to me. You will see next to it, the next picture is the same street as it is now. And that street was in the medieval times and before very much occupied by Jews. There are two synagogues, which, in the last 20, 30 years, have been completely rehabilitated or actually archaeologically refurbished. Showing one of the synagogues, which was dating back to 1050 or 1100, which is the only comparable replica is the Rashi synagogue in [? Worms. ?] The final picture which we're coming to is a Palatinus Hotel, which is on the same side, showing the right-hand side of the street, whereas I was showing the left-hand side of the street. As you see this street, this was the street where we were taken into the ghetto, which was really literally the town center, which was the old Roman part of it. I would like to just express my gratitude to my late cousin, who died, unfortunately, two years ago, Professor Gershon Weiler. That a great deal of the information he has researched. And he had a lifelong [INAUDIBLE], being very upset about the Holocaust. He wasn't personally deported. He was in [? Munka Tabor. ?] And finally, when he died, I realized the pain he went through because he left it behind that on his gravestone should be put that he's the son of his parents and brother of the sister who perished in the Holocaust, and he could never console himself of his loss. This is true. This is a document, which is the copy of the original German document in the lager of Buchenwald, which we were taken by similar people who were inmates and who had to write out all our details on arrival from Auschwitz. And it goes as follows. First of all, it says Konzentrationslager Buchenwald, and gives immediately on the top the lager number, which was 66,226. This is my own details. It took me quite a long time to research it in the archives of Yad Vashem. Then, it goes on to say Weiler, family name, [INAUDIBLE], the date of birth, which is given as 14/3/1928. The day and the month is correct. The year is wrong. It was 1930. I had to make myself older, or I would have never been taken in a work camp. Then it carries on to say the date-- the place I come from-- Sopron in Hungarian, Odenburg in German. Then it gives the address, our home address, in Sopron, [INAUDIBLE] 26. Then it gives you my occupation-- I'm just translating from German-- schuler. That means I was a student. And then, which country I belong to was Hungary, [INAUDIBLE]. Then what my religion is-- Jewish. Then the other personal details as regards to color of eyes, shape of face, any abnormal marks or so on. Then it carries on to give the-- then it carries on and gives you your height, the color of your eyes, color of your hair, what languages you speak-- I spoke German and Hungarian. Then, the further details did not all apply to me-- whether I was married or not. Obviously, I wasn't. And then it says number 1. [GERMAN]. That means [? 'being ?] delivered' in Kat Zet, Auschwitz on 8/7/44, exact date. Then, number 2, eingellefert-- that means delivered-- on the 16th-- sorry, 8th of July, I should have said. 8th of July, '44. Then it says in the Kat Zet Auschwitz. Then eingellefert number 2 is 16th of July '44 from Kat Zet Auschwitz. In other words, there was a perfectly good order. And then all the other points didn't apply to me. And on the left-hand corner-- it may be visible-- is my signature. In fact, my only signature which I have from the age of 14 and some months. And it's interesting to note that on the bottom there is a warning that there is a serious--

speaker: interviewer
text: We've run out of tape, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Goodbye. I just wanted to tell you--
